title: Remove Minimum Number of Characters from Both Strings

statement.markdown: Given two strings `s1` and `s2`, find the minimum number of operations (insertions, deletions, or substitutions) required to make both strings equal.
statement.inputFormat: Two space-separated integers representing the sizes of the two strings, followed by the two strings themselves, each on a new line.
statement.outputFormat: The minimum number of operations required to make the two strings equal.

examples.explanation: To make 'abcba' equal to 'bbbbb', we need to substitute the first 'a' with 'b'.

hints.text: Use dynamic programming to find the minimum number of operations.

editorial.title: Minimum Edit Distance
editorial.intuition: To find the minimum number of operations, we can use dynamic programming to build a 2D matrix where each cell [i][j] represents the minimum number of operations required to make the first i characters of the first string equal to the first j characters of the second string.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a brute force approach to try all possible operations (insertions, deletions, or substitutions) for each position in the strings.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use dynamic programming to build the matrix in a bottom-up manner, starting from the base cases where one of the strings is empty.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a 2D matrix to store the minimum number of operations required to make the first i characters of the first string equal to the first j characters of the second string.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can prove the correctness of the solution by showing that the matrix is built correctly using the right formula.
editorial.dryRun: We can perform a dry run with sample inputs to verify the correctness of the solution.

functionSignature.cpp: int solve(const std::string& s1, const std::string& s2);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, s1: str, s2: str) -> int:
